metric_value: 'Any business of any size can now run its enterprise applications on Microsoft software and industry-standard hardware' — Bill Gates at Scalability Day, New York
source_page: Microsoft to World: We Want It All
notes: Multi-million dollar event; Gates opening declaration.

metric_value: Aberdeen: NT jumped from workgroup to departmental (~250 users) by Nov 1995; NT Server 4.0 'a giant step forward' July 1996
source_page: Microsoft to World: We Want It All
notes: Kastner acknowledges Aberdeen's own bullish prior coverage of NT's progress.

metric_value: Single two-processor Pentium Pro with NT and IIS processed 100M hits in 24 hours — one-third greater than Microsoft's own site traffic
notes: Kastner's critique: unrealistic load distribution (even over 24 hours); challenge: 'let Microsoft run its HTTP traffic at www.microsoft.com on one server.'

metric_value: 'Demonstrates performance of computer system large enough to manage daily banking activities of a quarter of the world's population'
notes: Kastner's critique: benchmark has features of obsolete TPC-A test; 'far cry from a true bank application's functionality.'

metric_name: 1.8M emails/day with 50,000 users per server benchmark
metric_value: Exchange Server benchmark; all Internet POP3 and SMTP; workload spread over 24 hours; product not shipping until Q1-1998
notes: Kastner's critique: ignores normal email peaks; customers report inability to scale beyond a few hundred Exchange clients.

metric_value: Microsoft to sell satellite images over Internet starting October using future SQL Server version
notes: Kastner's critique: all SQL Server competitors already supported large databases and relational objects; 'No big deal.'

metric_value: Future release of SQL Server on future release of NT will support Very Large Memories
notes: Kastner's critique: Oracle and Informix have done this for over a year on Digital Unix/Alpha. 'OK, but...'

metric_value: Oracle and Informix supported VLM on Digital Equipment's Unix on Alpha for over a year before Microsoft's announcement
notes: Aberdeen's competitive context showing Microsoft was following Oracle/Informix, not leading.

metric_value: NT and BackOffice require capable partner help for enterprise-class: high transaction rates, high availability, fault tolerance, mainframe connectivity, systems management
source_page: For Now, Look to Microsoft's Partners
notes: Kastner: Microsoft 'schizophrenic about partnering, wanting all kudos and revenue but unable to supply best-of-breed in all categories.'

metric_value: Intel microprocessor performance improvements given 'scant credit' for NT scalability at Scalability Day
source_page: For Now, Look to Microsoft's Partners
notes: Kastner implies NT's scalability improvements were partly driven by faster Intel chips, not software improvements alone.

metric_value: Unstated reason for Scalability Day: address continuing NT scalability debacles where line-of-business projects push NT beyond real-world capabilities
notes: Kastner's cynical read: the event was damage control, not product launch.

metric_value: NT will be 'better in 1998 than it is now' but remains best suited to departmental applications; unable to scale to Fortune 500 enterprise loads in mid-1997
source_page: Bottom Line: NT is Still 'Not There'
notes: Kastner's balanced assessment: NT improving but not enterprise-ready in 1997.

metric_value: 'Real enterprise high-end legitimately remains the province of Unix, the AS/400, and even the mainframe'
source_page: Bottom Line: NT is Still 'Not There'
notes: Kastner's bottom-line enterprise platform positioning as of mid-1997.

metric_value: Aberdeen had 'considerable difficulty finding sites with more than a few hundred users' on NT
source_page: Bottom Line: NT is Still 'Not There'
notes: Empirical basis for enterprise-scale limitation; also customer reports of Exchange scaling beyond a few hundred clients.

metric_value: Scalability Day demonstrations will 'distance the company even further from scalability reality' and 'open up a hard-to-close trust gap'
notes: Kastner's prediction about long-term marketing damage from overpromising.

metric_value: NT scalability will improve; many world's applications are now NT-capable but enterprise high-end requires Unix/AS400/mainframe for now
source_page: Bottom Line: NT is Still 'Not There'
notes: Kastner hedges: NT is improving but not yet enterprise-class in 1997.

metric_value: Windows 2000/2003 substantially improved enterprise scalability; NT Server licenses grew 50% faster than competitors in 1998 per IDC; Windows eventually captured server market share from Unix but Unix/Linux maintained enterprise high-end into 2000s; Linux won server market by 2010s
notes: Sources: Microsoft press release Jan 1999 (IDC data); CRN 2005 Windows-Unix parity; StatCounter 2026. Kastner correct that NT was not ready in 1997 but underestimated Windows 2000's improvement.

metric_value: Exchange 2000/2003 substantially improved scalability beyond hundreds-of-user limitation; became dominant enterprise email platform with 400M+ seats; Kastner's scalability concern proved temporary
notes: Exchange grew to dominate enterprise email despite 1997 scalability limitations. Kastner's concern was valid in 1997 but short-lived.

metric_value: Unix maintained enterprise dominance through 2005; Windows servers achieved revenue parity with Unix by 2005 per IDC; Linux ultimately displaced both Unix and Windows NT in data centers by 2010s; Kastner's Unix defense proved correct for 2000-2005 window
notes: Sources: CRN 2005 Unix-Windows parity; StatCounter 2026. Kastner's Unix defense proved more prescient than Microsoft's claims.

metric_value: Microsoft pursuing 'all-Microsoft solutions' strategy from desktop to enterprise despite partner dependencies; schizophrenic about partnering
source_page: For Now, Look to Microsoft's Partners
notes: Kastner's structural critique of Microsoft's platform monopoly strategy.

metric_value: 'Scant help for executives designing for today's IT projects' — much of Scalability Day demonstrations depended on next year's software
notes: Kritik of Microsoft's vaporware-heavy Scalability Day presentation.

metric_value: 'Let's not be distracted by marketing hype from reality — or caught by the Big Lie'
source_page: Microsoft to World: We Want It All
notes: Kastner's most provocative framing — invoking 'Big Lie' concept against Microsoft's enterprise claims.
